blocks.text: He did what was right by reforming the people, and removing the wicked abominations.
He kept his heart fixed on the Lord; in lawless times he made godliness prevail.
Except for David and Hezekiah and Josiah, all of them were great sinners, for they abandoned the law of the Most High; the kings of Judah came to an end.
They gave their power to others, and their glory to a foreign nation,
who set fire to the chosen city of the sanctuary, and made its streets desolate, as Jeremiah had foretold.
For they had mistreated him, who even in the womb had been consecrated a prophet, to pluck up and ruin and destroy, and likewise to build and to plant.
4–7 : A summary of the Babylonian destruction of Jerusalem in 586 . As in the Deuteronomistic History, only these three kings were fully righteous.
It was Ezekiel who saw the vision of glory, which God showed him above the chariot of the cherubim.
For God also mentioned Job who held fast to all the ways of justice.
9 : See and cf. Ezek 14.14,20.
May the bones of the Twelve Prophets send forth new life from where they lie, for they comforted the people of Jacob and delivered them with confident hope.
10 : Ben Sira knows the Twelve Minor Prophets (Hosea–Malachi) as one book; cf. 46.12.
How shall we magnify Zerubbabel? He was like a signet ring on the right hand,
and so was Jeshua son of Jozadak; in their days they built the house and raised a temple holy to the Lord, destined for everlasting glory.
The memory of Nehemiah also is lasting; he raised our fallen walls, and set up gates and bars, and rebuilt our ruined houses.
49.1–13: Josiah and succeeding ancestors.
11–13 : Postexilic leaders: (Ezra 3.2; Hag 2.23), (Ezra 3.2; Hag 1.12; Zech 3; 6.9–13), (Nehemiah, esp. 2.17–3.32).
Few have ever been created on earth like Enoch, for he was taken up from the earth.
Nor was anyone ever born like Joseph; even his bones were cared for.
Shem and Seth and Enosh were honored, but above every other created living being was Adam.

44.1–50.24: Praise of Israel's ancestors. A long section in which Ben Sira celebrates significant figures from Israel's past, who were rewarded with a lasting name. He emphasizes covenant along with the offices of prophet, priest, and king, concluding with praise of a contemporary, the high priest Simon II.
49.14–16: Concluding ancestors. A conclusion that extends back to the first human.
